title: Epic: host-asterinas-workspace — bring the asterinas planner onto jjforge
body:
Goal: host the asterinas-workspace project (~968
markdown-file "issues" under `~/p/asterinas-workspace/issues/`)
on jjforge. End state: `jjf ready` against the asterinas
planner picks up the right next ticket; subagents work it;
the markdown tree becomes a read-only archive.

# Approach

We're explicitly bending asterinas to fit jjforge — NOT
the other way around. The asterinas tree today is a loose
markdown convention with ad-hoc per-phase taxonomies. The
ambition is to replace that with a proper queryable surface,
using beads as the design pattern for "what features does
a working agent-driven planner need." Wherever a beads
feature would express an asterinas convention better than
ours does today, we add the feature to jjforge.

Three phases.

## Phase 1: feature audit against beads

Walk the asterinas issues end-to-end. For each convention
(`Status: workaround-in-place`, `Workflow: [host+qemu]`,
phase/epic hierarchy, "Lane: A/B", `Blocked by:`,
provenance like "filed from epic XYZ's out-of-scope list"),
ask:

- What's the cleanest jjforge representation today?
- Does beads have a primitive that would express it
  better? (`bd remember`, `bd dep --kind discovered-from`,
  `bd ready --priority`, hierarchical IDs, `bd metadata`,
  `bd defer`, `bd hooked`, etc.)
- If so: is the cost/benefit right to add that primitive
  to jjforge?

Output: a markdown audit doc enumerating each asterinas
convention, the current jjforge mapping, the beads-feature
candidate, and a verdict (ship the feature / use a label /
skip).

## Phase 2: implement the features the audit picks

The audit's outputs become tickets. We file and ship them
**before** migrating. Each gets its own implementation
ticket under this epic OR under
`epic:agent-ergonomics` if it's general agent-shape work.

The two siblings we just shipped (`agent-claim-atomic`,
`agent-dep-types`) are the precedent. Schema bumps land in
sequence (v2.6 → v2.7 etc.), each adds the specific feature
the audit identified.

## Phase 3: migration via Haiku fan-out

Once the schema can express every asterinas convention
queriably, fan out a swarm of Haiku agents — many small,
parallel imports — over disjoint slices of the markdown
tree. Each agent reads files, applies the audit's mapping
rules, emits `jjf new` calls.

The Haiku tier is right for this: cheap, fast, good
enough for "read a markdown file and call `jjf new` with
the right fields." We're not asking it to design anything
— the audit doc IS the design.

# Child tickets we'll file under this epic

- `host-asterinas-audit` (research): the feature audit.
  Read every asterinas convention; compare to beads;
  produce the gap analysis + recommendation doc.
- `host-asterinas-features` (feature, sketched): umbrella
  for the schema bumps the audit identifies. Concrete
  per-feature tickets get filed once we know the list.
- `host-asterinas-migrate` (feature): the Haiku fan-out
  migration. Depends on the feature tickets shipping.

# Depends on

- `61e9a1c storage-snapshot-cache` (CLOSED) — the perf
  unblock. At ~968 issues, the per-issue jj-log read path
  was 22.5s; the cache makes steady-state 15ms. Without
  this, the migration would be DOA.
- `b6d066b agent-dep-types` (CLOSED) — parent-child edges
  let us express phase → epic → ticket hierarchy properly.
  Use this epic's children as the first real test.

# Blocks

- The "project agent" speculation in `585b84b` is the
  next logical step AFTER this epic ships: once asterinas
  is on jjforge, an agent loop running against `jjf ready`
  becomes the live test of the orchestration thesis.

# Status

Filed 2026-06-23. Sketched. First child to file:
`host-asterinas-audit`.
